summary: India is building a national quantum-safe ecosystem coordinated through the Department of Science and Technology under the National Quantum Mission, whose task force has set out a strategy for migration to post-quantum cryptography. CERT-In and MeitY have issued a transition roadmap for public and private organisations, the Telecommunication Engineering Centre has published a technical report for the telecom sector, and SEBI requires its regulated entities to run post-quantum risk assessments and maintain cryptographic-asset inventories.
govActors:
DST (National Quantum Mission) | Department of Science and Technology, sets national quantum-safe migration strategy through its task force
CERT-In | Indian Computer Emergency Response Team, issued the quantum cyber readiness transition roadmap
MeitY | Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology, co-author of the quantum-safe cybersecurity whitepaper
TEC | Telecommunication Engineering Centre, published the technical report on migration to post-quantum cryptography
SEBI | Securities and Exchange Board of India, requires post-quantum risk assessment and cryptographic-asset inventories of regulated entities
provenance: Lead institution + national strategy: DST National Quantum Mission task force report and Quantum-Safe Ecosystem report (both ingested)
CERT-In + MeitY roadmap: CERT-In whitepaper CIWP-2025-0002 and PIB/MeitY release (ingested)
TEC telecom framework: TEC 910018:2025 technical report (ingested)
SEBI inventories + risk assessment: SEBI CSCRF circular 2024 and 2025 technical clarifications (ingested)

hybridDeployment Recommended: Quantum-Safe Ecosystem report summary describes hybrid deployment frameworks; recorded as Recommended - REVIEW (the documents describe hybrid frameworks but the explicit mandatory/recommended stance is not quoted in summaries)

dominantProcess OMITTED: documents describe migration to post-quantum / quantum-safe cryptography and a national testing-and-certification framework but do not name the NIST FIPS suite nor an indigenous algorithm scheme, so the camp is not clearly attested
migrationTimeline OMITTED: task force describes phased timelines for critical information infrastructure but no specific years are stated in the summaries; targetCompletion likewise not stated
RBIH banking whitepaper excluded (included:false, non-government S3 host, RBI subsidiary)

dominantProcess re-classified NIST by best-available signal (medium): DST/CERT-In/MeitY/TEC/SEBI documents drive migration to post-quantum cryptography following international PQC standards, with no indigenous national algorithm scheme; the Atmanirbhar framing and national testing/certification framework concern deployment self-reliance rather than a sovereign algorithm, so NIST is the de facto default. Provisional, flagged for review
Model 2026-06-14 (NIST-bloc / contextualiser, Medium): DST/MeitY/CERT-In anchor validation on NIST CAVP; national certification overlay, not a national algorithm.

mainRegulation:
SEBI CSCRF cybersecurity framework (securities market) | national | binding-law
CERT-In / MeitY post-quantum migration roadmap | national | guidance
NIST CAVP algorithm validation anchor | transnational | binding-by-market-access
obligation: Regulated securities-market entities must meet the SEBI cybersecurity framework; the CERT-In/MeitY roadmap anchors algorithm validation on the NIST CAVP. India is building a national certification overlay, not a national algorithm.
analyticalNote: India adopts the NIST baseline with a national certification overlay, binding in the securities sector via SEBI. Watch point: indigenous ambitions exist but no national algorithm is mandated.
